explanation:
**Technical calculators** for IT: latency, storage, uptime SLA, cloud costs. Decisions that scale.

## How It's Calculated

Estimated Docker image size based on your chosen base OS. This calculator applies the formula with your inputs, checking typical ranges and alerting you to unusual values.

## Final Notes

This calculation is a reference guide. For critical decisions (financial, medical, legal), consult a qualified professional. Values are current as of 2026 and reviewed regularly.

faq.q: What's the best Docker base image size?
faq.a: Alpine is smallest (~5 MB), but glibc compatibility issues can occur. Debian slim offers a middle ground at ~70 MB with better compatibility. Distroless (~20 MB) is most secure with minimal attack surface but lacks a shell.
Build your app in one image, then copy only artifacts to a slim base. This dramatically reduces final image size by excluding build tools and dependencies.

faq.q: When should I use Distroless images?
faq.a: Distroless images contain only your app and runtime—no shell or package manager. They're more secure and lighter, but harder to debug. Best for production workloads.
Without .dockerignore, Docker copies unnecessary files into your image, bloating its size. Always exclude node_modules, .git, test files, and other artifacts.

faq.q: How do I scan Docker images for vulnerabilities?
faq.a: Use tools like Trivy or Snyk to detect CVEs in your images. Many can integrate into your CI/CD pipeline for automatic scanning on every build.
Yes, all our calculators are completely free and require no signup.
These are approximate sizes based on standard base images. Actual sizes may vary slightly based on your specific dependencies and configuration. For critical decisions, always test with real images.

faq.q: Does this tool store my data?
faq.a: No. All calculations happen in your browser. We don't send any data to servers.
